According to the Companies Act, 2013 (not the Company Secretaries Act, 1980, which regulates the profession but not the mandatory appointment), the requirement for appointing a whole-time company secretary is outlined in Section 203 and related rules (such as Rule 8 and Rule 8A of the Companies (Appointment and Remuneration of Managerial Personnel) Rules, 2014).

Specifically:
- All listed companies must appoint a company secretary, regardless of their paid-up share capital[[1]](https://ca2013.com/203-appointment-of-key-managerial-personnel/).
- Other public companies with paid-up share capital of ₹10 crore or more must appoint full key managerial personnel, including a company secretary[[1]](https://ca2013.com/203-appointment-of-key-managerial-personnel/).
- Private companies with paid-up share capital of ₹10 crore or more must appoint a whole-time company secretary[[1]](https://ca2013.com/203-appointment-of-key-managerial-personnel/)[[2]](https://www.legitquest.com/act/companies-appointment-and-remuneration-of-managerial-personnel-amendment-rules-2020/90e8).

As of February 2026, there appears to be a proposal to raise the threshold to ₹25 crore, but it has not been implemented yet, so the current threshold remains ₹10 crore[[3]](https://www.patronaccounting.com/blog/company-secretary-role-appointment-and-removal-under-companies-act-2013)[[4]](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ca-dileep-reddy-bh-978a81174_bhdandassociates-mcaupdate-companysecretary-activity-7415379487116156928-uiM5).

Evaluating the options:
- (A) is partially correct but incomplete, as it doesn't cover non-listed companies meeting the capital threshold.
- (B) and (C) are incorrect; turnover or employee count are not the criteria for mandatory CS appointment (they may relate to other compliances like secretarial audit).
- (D) matches the key threshold specified in the rules for both public and private companies (and practically covers most listed ones due to listing requirements).
- (E) is incorrect; not all private limited companies need a CS—only those meeting the capital threshold.

The most accurate option based on the current law is (D).

<final_answer>
(D)
</final_answer>
